fields.brief_block: Specifically, deadly force is justified if the individual reasonably believes it is necessary to prevent death or great bodily injury to themselves or a third person, or to prevent a forcible felony. Prayor v. State, 217 Ga. App. 56, OCGA § 16-3-21, Chambers v. State, 308 Ga. App. 748.
fields.opinion_block: [A] person is justified in using force which is intended or likely to cause death or great bodily harm only if he or she reasonably believes that such force is necessary to prevent death or great bodily injury to himself or herself or a third person or to prevent the commission of a forcible felony.
fields.finding_analysis: Chambers quotes OCGA § 16-3-21 (a) and applies it as the controlling justification standard, including the precise rule that force intended or likely to cause death or great bodily harm is justified only on a reasonable belief that it is necessary to prevent death or great bodily injury to oneself or a third person, or to prevent a forcible felony. That statutory language tracks the brief's proposition almost word for word, so the cited case supports the stated principle.
